paper_title: Agentic Aggregation for Parallel Scaling of Long-Horizon Agentic Tasks

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We study parallel test-time scaling for long-horizon agentic tasks such as agentic search and deep research, where multiple rollouts are generated in parallel and aggregated into a final response. While such scaling has proven effective for chain-of-thought reasoning, agentic tasks pose unique challenges: trajectories are long, multi-turn, and tool-augmented, and outputs are often open-ended. Aggregating only final answers discards rich information from trajectories, while concatenating all trajectories exceeds the model's context window. To address this, we propose AggAgent, an aggregation agent that treats parallel trajectories as an environment. We equip it with lightweight tools to inspect candidate solutions and search across trajectories, enabling it to navigate and synthesize information on demand. Across six benchmarks and three model families (GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5, MiniMax-M2.5), AggAgent outperforms all existing aggregation methods-by up to 5.3% absolute on average and 10.3% on two deep research tasks-while adding minimal overhead, as the aggregation cost remains bounded by a single agentic rollout. Our findings establish agentic aggregation as an effective and cost-efficient approach to parallel test-time scaling.

sections.content: We study parallel test-time scaling for long-horizon agentic tasks such as agentic search and deep research, where multiple rollouts are generated in parallel and aggregated into final response. sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Scaling test-time compute has emerged as promising avenue for enhancing the performance of large language models (LLMs) (Wei et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Brown et al., 2024; Welleck et al., 2024; Snell et al., 2025; Muennighoff et al., 2025). This success has largely been demonstrated in standard chain-of-thought (CoT) tasks such as mathematical reasoning and coding (Qi et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2025). However, long-horizon agentic tasks 6 2 0 2 3 1 ] . [ 1 3 5 7 1 1 . 4 0 6 2 : r Figure 1: AggAgent consistently outperforms existing aggregation methods. We measure the average performance across six long-horizon agentic benchmarks (Section 4) against the number of parallel trajectories. (Top) An agent produces = 3 independent rollouts on long-horizon task. (Bottom) Solution Aggregation feeds only final solutions to an LLM, discarding intermediate reasoning. Summary Aggregation compresses each trajectory into lossy summary. AggAgent (ours) navigates trajectories via tools in an agentic manner, enabling full-fidelity cross-trajectory reasoning at low cost. (e.g., deep research, software engineering, web navigation) present fundamentally different challenge: trajectories are multi-turn, spanning hundreds of steps with interleaved tool calls and observations (Yao et al., 2022b). In this work, we study parallel scaling for long-horizon agentic tasks, an approach proven effective for CoT reasoning tasks yet largely unexplored for agentic search and deep research. Parallel scaling enables simultaneous generation of multiple independent trajectories, offering natural computational advantage (Zhao et al., 2025). Furthermore, parallel scaling offers substantial improvement over single-agent performance (Wei et al., 2025; Li et al., 2026): for instance, GLM-4.7-Flash (Zeng et al., 2025) improves from 27% to 59% on BrowseComp (Wei et al., 2025) and 25% to 51% on HLE (Phan et al., 2025) when scaling from Pass@1 to Pass@8, confirming that correct solutions frequently exist within the parallel rollouts. The central question then becomes how to effectively aggregate these trajectories. While voting and solution aggregation methods have proven effective for mathematical reasoning and coding (Wang et al., 2023; Fu et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2025), long-horizon agentic tasks pose distinct challenges: evidence is sparse and distributed across multi-turn trajectories, demanding reasoning far beyond shallow heuristics and final solutions (Li et al., 2025a). Furthermore, individual trajectories may each capture only partial progress toward complex task, requiring cross-trajectory synthesis to assemble complete solution (Chang et al., 2026). However, existing approaches fall short  (Table 1)  : aggregating only final solutions discards the rich information in trajectories, summarizing trajectories is expensive and lossy, and concatenating all trajectories into single context is infeasible, as each trajectory spans hundreds of thousands of tokens. To address these challenges, we propose AggAgent, which frames aggregation itself as an agentic task, treating the set of trajectories as an environment to interact with (Figure 2). AggAgent interacts with trajectories on demand through three lightweight tools: get_solution (retrieve final solutions of one or all trajectories), search_trajectory (keyword search within trajectory), and get_segment (read specific range of steps). Since these tools operate entirely over an array of completed trajectoriesrather than invoking external services like web search or code executionthey incur none of the API cost or latency of such external tools. This agentic aggregation allows for cross-trajectory reasoning and synthesis without the cost of loading all trajectories into context, preserving full fidelity while keeping the aggregation cost bounded by single agentic rollout. Furthermore, AggAgent is Pareto-optimal in cost and performance (Figure 3), adding only 5.7% overhead over the cost of running 8 parallel agentscompared to 41% for Summary Aggregationby selectively reading portions of trajectories rather than loading them entirely into context. sections.title: 2 Problem Formulation
sections.content: We study long-horizon tasks such as agentic search and deep research. Given problem q, the goal is to produce an output y, which may be either short answer, set of answers, or long-form report. An agent interacts with an external environment to solve by generating trajectory = (q, r1, a1, o1, . . . , rm, am, om, y), where each step consists of internal thinking rj, tool call aj, and resulting observation oj. In parallel scaling, we run the same agent independently for times, yielding = {T1, T2, . . . , TK}. This setting is natural in production deployments of frontier systems (Anthropic, 2025; Cursor, 2025; Kimi Team et al., 2026), as the latency is determined by the slowest of the rollouts. The core problem centers around how to aggregate these trajectories and synthesize better solution y. We formalize aggregation as function : (q, ) (cid:55) ˆy, where ˆy is the aggregated final solution. For agentic search, ˆy is evaluated against the ground-truth answer y, while for deep research, it is evaluated against problem-specific rubrics. Existing methods We adopt the following aggregation methods from prior works: Majority Voting (MV) (Wang et al., 2023): selects the most frequent solution. However, voting methods do not work for multi-answer or long-form generation tasks. Best-of-N (BoN) (Cobbe et al., 2021; Uesato et al., 2022): selects the solution with the highest self-reported confidence. While reward model scores are commonly used as the weight for CoT reasoning tasks, recent works on agentic tasks have adopted the models self-reported confidence as the weight (Wei et al., 2025; Zhu et al., 2026). Weighted Majority Voting (WMV) (Li et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2024): selects the solution with the highest total weight, where each solution is weighted by self-reported confidence. Fewest Tool Calls (FewTool) (Liu et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2025): selects the solution from the trajectory that required the fewest tool calls. Solution Aggregation (SolAgg) (Qi et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2025; Qiao et al., 2025): concatenates all solutions and prompts an LLM to synthesize final solution. Summary Aggregation (SummAgg) (Li et al., 2025a; Hu et al., 2026): compresses each trajectory into summary report, concatenates all reports, and prompts an LLM to generate final solution. We classify these baselines into two groups. Heuristic methods (MV, WMV, BoN, FewTool) rely on shallow signals such as answer frequency, confidence scores, or trajectory length, without inspecting trajectory content. LLM-based methods (SolAgg, SummAgg) leverage LLM reasoning for solution synthesis but remain limited: Solution Aggregation aggregates only final solutions, discarding all intermediate evidence, while Summary Aggregation compresses each trajectory into summary report before aggregation, incurring irreversible information loss and requiring additional LLM calls. Table 1 summarizes these limitations.

sections.title: 3 Our Approach: AggAgent
sections.content: To address the aforementioned limitations, we propose AggAgent, where the aggregator interacts with directly in an agentic manner. The initial user message provides the problem and trajectory metadata, which consists of the number of steps, total token count, and tool usage statistics for each trajectory Ti. The trajectories themselves are not pre-loaded into the context but reside in the environment and are retrieved on-demand, keeping the aggregation cost bounded by single context window independent of K. This design enables cross-trajectory reasoning at full fidelity and low cost  (Table 1)  . Tool design We provide AggAgent with four tools as follows2: get_solution(traj_id): Retrieves the final solution from each trajectorys last step. Unless traj_id is specified, returns all solutions by default. search_trajectory(traj_id, query, role, k): Searches for keywords within single trajectory. Returns the top matching steps ranked by ROUGE-L score (Lin, 2004). get_segment(traj_id, start_step, end_step): Reads the full content of contiguous range of steps from single trajectory. Returns raw thinking and tool observations for the specified window. finish(): Submits the final solution along with reason for the aggregation. Workflow The tool design reflects natural coarse-to-fine investigative workflow. Rather than reading every trajectory in full, the agent first surveys the metadata and solutions loaded by get_solution to identify consensus and disagreements across the rollouts, and to pinpoint trajectories worth closer inspection. It then selectively dives into individual trajectories via search_trajectory and get_segment to verify key claims against thinking blocks and tool observations. After sufficient cross-trajectory validation, it calls finish to submit the final synthesized solution. We provide the full prompts in Appendix B. Cost analysis Aggregation cost is measured in additional LLM calls beyond the rollouts. Heuristic methods require zero additional calls, while Solution Aggregation requires single LLM call to synthesize the solution candidates. Summary Aggregation is the most expensive, as it requires separate LLM calls before aggregation, each of which can be as long as the maximum context window. In contrast, AggAgents total context is bounded by single context window of the model, keeping aggregation cost independent of and comparable to Solution Aggregation. Moreover, as AggAgents tools operate entirely over the in-memory trajectory array, they incur none of the latency or cost of external tools like web search and code execution.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Tasks We evaluate on six long-horizon agentic tasks broadly in two categories: 1) Agentic search: BrowseComp (Wei et al., 2025) consists of challenging factual questions demanding exhaustive multi-step web browsing; BrowseComp-Plus (Chen et al., 2025) enables controlled evaluation on BrowseComp questions by replacing web search with local knowledge base; HLE (Phan et al., 2025) covers expert-level questions across diverse academic disciplines, emphasizing rigorous reasoning; DeepSearchQA (Gupta et al., 2026) 2We provide the full tool descriptions in Figure 18 in the appendix. 4 targets multi-answer queries where completeness across all valid answers is required. 2) Deep research: Healthbench-Hard (Arora et al., 2025) requires generating comprehensive long-form responses to challenging medical queries; ResearchRubrics (Sharma et al., 2025) poses open-ended research tasks evaluated against detailed, multi-criterion rubrics. Due to high costs of long-horizon rollouts, we randomly sample subsets for evaluation3. For HLE, we use 155 search-focused questions (Li et al., 2025a). For ResearchRubrics, we use the full 101 problems. For Healthbench-Hard, we randomly sample 100 instances. For all other tasks, we randomly sample 150 questions following Sun et al. (2025); Zhang et al. (2025a). Models We employ three model families of varying sizes for base trajectory rollouts: GLM-4.7-Flash (30B) (Zeng et al., 2025), Qwen3.5-122B-A10B (122B) (Qwen Team, 2026), and MiniMax-M2.5 (229B) (MiniMax, 2026). We adopt Tongyi DeepResearch (Tongyi DeepResearch Team et al., 2025) as the agent scaffold, use native function calling, allow at most 128K context length with 100 tool calls, and sample 8 independent trajectories. For LLM-based aggregation, we use the same model as the rollout agent. Rollout agent tools For BrowseComp-Plus, we follow the official implementation and provide search and get_document tools using Qwen3-Embedding-8B (Zhang et al., 2025b) as the retriever. For all other tasks, we provide search and visit tools following prior work (Yen et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025b; Wu et al., 2025; Gao et al., 2026), where search uses Serper API4 for Google search and visit uses crawl4ai5 to scrape specific web page. Cost and latency We report total cost and latency per query. Cost includes rollout, tool calls, and aggregation, while latency includes rollout and aggregation. Evaluation We employ LLM-as-a-judge, following the official evaluation setting. Specifically, we use Qwen3.5-397B-A17B for ResearchRubrics and GPT-4.1 (Achiam et al., 2023) for all other datasets. We perform bootstrapped sampling for calculating Metric@K {1, 2, 4, 8}. For BrowseComp, BrowseComp-Plus, and HLE, the predicted answer ˆy is evaluated against the gold answer y. For DeepSearchQA, the predicted answer set is considered correct if it exactly matches the gold answer set. For Healthbench-Hard and ResearchRubrics, the long-form response is evaluated across multiple problem-specific rubrics, and the scores are averaged. The rubrics contain negative scores, hence the total score could also be negative. We provide further experimental details in Appendix A.

sections.title: 5.1 Main Results
sections.content: We present the main results on = 8 in Table 2, and present the full results (K {1, 2, 4, 8}) in Appendix C.1. AggAgent consistently outperforms all baselines across all three models, improving over Pass@1 by 13.317.9 points and over the strongest baseline (SolAgg) by 2.45.3 points in average score. Among the heuristic baselines, Majority Voting performs the worst, while confidencebased methods (WMV, BoN) yield substantial improvements, consistent with prior observations (Wei et al., 2025). However, the gains are less pronounced on DeepSearchQA, Healthbench-Hard, and ResearchRubrics, where the model is poorly calibrated (see Appendix C.4 for details). Notably, voting methods are not applicable to multi-answer or long-form tasks, so their performance gains are limited to single-answer tasks only.6 Fewest Tool Calls has been popular on BrowseComp due to its simplicity (Liu et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2025), but our results show that it also does not generalize well beyond such tasks. 3We report standard deviation in Appendix C.1. 4https://serper.dev 5https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai 6We use Pass@1 instead to calculate the average score.

While no single baseline is universally optimal, AggAgent achieves strong performance by enabling fine-grained reasoning over trajectories without sacrificing detail or coherence.

sections.title: 5.2 Cost and Latency vs. Performance
sections.content: We compare cost and latency versus performance for different numbers of samples {2, 4, 8} in Figure 3 (full breakdown in Appendix C.2). As increases, all methods become more expensive and slower due to additional rollouts. AggAgent and Solution Aggregation introduce only minimal overhead beyond heuristic baselinesat K=8, their overhead over the rollout cost is 5.7% and 3.7%, respectivelyyet substantially outperform them. Interestingly, their overhead does not increase proportionally to K, suggesting that having more samples can sometimes make aggregation easier by providing stronger supporting evidence, partially offsetting the extra computation spent on harder instances. In contrast, Summary Aggregation incurs 41% overhead at K=8 because it must compress each trajectory before aggregation. Overall, AggAgent achieves Pareto-optimal performance and efficiency across benchmarks, showing that agentic aggregation scales efficiently with test-time compute without incurring substantial additional cost.

sections.title: 6.1 Stronger Models for Aggregation
sections.content: Could aggregation benefit from employing stronger model? We investigate this by replacing the aggregator with MiniMax-M2.5 while keeping GLM-4.7-Flash as the base rollout agent. As shown in Figure 4 (full results in Table 11 in the appendix), using stronger aggregator generally improves performance, with MiniMax-based AggAgent achieving the highest average score and even surpassing Pass@8 on BrowseComp-Plus. This points to asymmetric model allocationa stronger model for aggregation, weaker models for parallel rolloutsas practical strategy for designing multi-agent systems, consistent with prior work employing stronger models as orchestrators over multiple weaker subagents (Qiao et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2025a; Akay et al., 2026). In our setting, the aggregator plays the orchestrating role, reasoning across parallel trajectories to produce final solution. Figure 4: Employing stronger aggregator improves performance on LLM-based aggregation methods. In all cases, GLM-4.7-Flash serves as the rollout agent; blue bars replace the aggregator with the stronger MiniMax-M2.5, while red bars use GLM-4.7-Flash for both roles. Yellow hatched bars denote the Pass@K. All methods are evaluated at K=8. 7 Figure 5: Ablation of solution synthesis vs. best-trajectory selection. AggAgent synthesizes new solution from the collected trajectories; the selection variant selects the single best trajectory directly. Color indicates model (red: GLM-4.7-Flash, green: Qwen3.5-122B, blue: MiniMax-M2.5); line style and marker indicate method (solid + circle: AggAgent, dashed + diamond: selection variant).

sections.title: 6.2 Output Design Ablation: Synthesis vs. Selection
sections.content: We ablate the output design of AggAgent by comparing it against selection variant, which selects the single best trajectory and adopts its solution directly instead of synthesizing new solution. As shown in Figure 5, AggAgent performs better overall, though the selection variant remains competitive in some settings. Notably, the selection variant performs poorly on deep research benchmarks, where AggAgent outperforms it by large margin. We attribute this to mismatch between selection and the nature of open-ended research tasks: quality is distributed across trajectories so no single trajectory dominates, yet selection forces an all-or-nothing commitment. Synthesis mitigates this by making local, compositional judgments about which parts of each trajectory to incorporate, rather than committing to global winner. This distinction is less pronounced for agentic search, where trajectories tend to be clearly correct or clearly wrong, making selection more straightforward. These results suggest that synthesis is often the preferable output design, consistent with our findings in Section 6.1 and 6.4 that AggAgent can compose correct solution from individually incorrect trajectories. We leave more thorough ablation of output design to future work.

sections.title: 6.3 Tool Usage Patterns
sections.content: In this section, we analyze the tool usage behaviour of AggAgent. As depicted in Figure 6, search_trajectory accounts for the majority of tool calls, while get_solution and finish are each invoked approximately once per query, consistent with the intended workflow: solutions are surveyed once at the start and the agent terminates after the final synthesis. Meanwhile, get_segment is used relatively less than search_trajectory, suggesting that AggAgent reserves full-content reads for cases where keyword-level search alone is insufficient, diving deep only when actually needed. We also observe that stronger models tend to require fewer tool calls overall, suggesting more capable models reach final solution more efficiently. Deep research tasks similarly exhibit fewer tool calls, potentially due to less need for resolving precise factual conflicts across trajectories. 8 Figure 6: Average number of tool calls per query by AggAgent. Numbers above each bar indicate the average total tool calls per query. search_trajectory dominates tool usage, while get_solution and finish are each called approximately once per query. get_segment is used more selectively, reflecting coarse-to-fine strategy where AggAgent commits to full-content reads only when keyword-level search is insufficient.

sections.title: 6.4 Qualitative Analysis
sections.content: We qualitatively analyze cases where AggAgent succeeds in producing correct answers. AggAgent demonstrates four key behaviours (Figure 7): (1) Minority answer identification: identifying the correct answer from multiple trajectories, even when it is held by only minority, (2) Disagreement resolution: resolving inconsistencies and conflicts across trajectories to arrive at coherent answer, (3) Cross-trajectory synthesis: constructing correct solution by reasoning over trajectories that are all incorrect, and (4) Heuristic interpretation: leveraging heuristic signals such as majority vote and confidence scores to guide aggregation, while not relying on them entirely. Together, these behaviours highlight AggAgents capacity for nuanced, evidence-driven reasoning across parallel trajectories beyond simple voting or selection.

sections.title: 7 Related Work
sections.content: Long-horizon agents LLM agents have emerged as powerful paradigm, extending language models beyond pure parametric generation to interactive problem solving in external environments. ReAct (Yao et al., 2022b) has been the most prominent workflow, interleaving reasoning, action selection, and environment observations. This has enabled wide range of applications, including agentic RAG (Jin et al., 2025b; Li et al., 2025c; Jin et al., 2025a), software engineering (Jimenez et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025), web navigation (Yao et al., 2022a; Chae et al., 2025; Gandhi & Neubig, 2026), and deep research systems (OpenAI, 2025; Anthropic, 2025; Roucher et al., 2025). Recent work has sought to improve agentic capabilities through advances in RL algorithms (Shao et al., 2024; 2025; Ritter et al., 2026), agentic mid-training (Su et al., 2025), and environment scaling (Fang et al., 2025). Our work is complementary to these efforts: we treat these agents as rollout models and study how to aggregate parallel trajectories into single high-quality solution. Test-time scaling Early work on test-time scaling for CoT tasks (Kojima et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022) such as math reasoning and coding showed clear gains from search-based strategies (Yao et al., 2023), extended thinking budgets (Muennighoff et al., 2025; Guo et al., 2025; Snell et al., 2025), majority voting (Wang et al., 2023; Brown et al., 2024), and learning-based aggregation (Qi et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2025). However, test-time scaling for long-horizon agents is more challenging, as trajectories are lengthy, heterogeneous, and difficult to compare using simple surface-level signals (e.g., majority or confidence). Parallel scaling with LLM-based aggregation mitigates this problem by generating multiple independent trajectories and reasoning over them to produce final solution. WebResearcher (Qiao et al., 2025) concatenates final answers and prompts an LLM 9 Figure 7: Qualitative examples illustrating four key behaviours of AggAgent. to synthesize solution, while ParallelMuse (Li et al., 2025a) compresses each trajectory into summary report before aggregation, incurring high cost and information loss. Concurrent with our work, KARL (Chang et al., 2026) feeds final answers back to the rollout agent with the same tools enabled, but it disregards full trajectories, and reusing rollout tools can be costly in practice. In contrast, AggAgent enables the aggregator to interact with trajectories in an agentic manner, supporting cross-trajectory reasoning without lossy compression or excessive cost. While AggAgent is training-free and effective with off-the-shelf LLMs, we leave fine-tuning the aggregator as promising direction for future work. Orthogonal to our focus on parallel scaling, several works have studied sequential scaling to extend the effective horizon of single agent through context management (Wu et al., 2025; Yen et al., 2025; Tang et al., 2025; Zeng et al., 2026), context folding (Sun et al., 2025; Ye et al., 2026), or iterative refinement (Zhu et al., 2026; Xiao et al., 2026).

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduce AggAgent, an aggregation agent for parallel scaling of long-horizon agentic tasks. By treating parallel trajectories as an interactive environment and navigating them via lightweight in-memory tools, AggAgent enables cross-trajectory reasoning at full fidelity, while avoiding both the information loss of compression and the prohibitive cost of loading all trajectories into context. Across six benchmarks and three models, AggAgent consistently outperforms all baselines, achieving Pareto-optimal performance and efficiency. Our findings establish agentic aggregation as principled and cost-efficient paradigm for parallel test-time scaling.

sections.title: Implementation
sections.content: We serve all models via vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) and use the Tongyi DeepResearch scaffold (Tongyi DeepResearch Team et al., 2025) for agentic workflow. We enable interleaved reasoning and native function calling, with temperature 1.0, top-p 0.95, 128K context window, 10K max output tokens, and at most 100 tool calls per rollout. Upon exhausting the context limit or tool call budget, the model is given one final opportunity to submit an answer without tool access. All experiments are conducted on 4H100 GPUs. 15 A.2 Rollout Agent Tools BrowseComp-Plus The local corpus contains 100,195 documents. search uses Qwen3Embedding-8B to retrieve the top-5 documents by score, with each snippet truncated to 512 tokens. get_document returns the full text of document by docid, truncated to 4,096 tokens following Su et al. (2025). search(query): Perform search on knowledge source. Returns top-5 hits with docid, score, and snippet. The snippet contains the documents contents (may be truncated based on token limits). get_document(docid): Retrieve full document by its docid. All other datasets We adopt the tool implementation from SLIM (Yen et al., 2025). search queries Google Search via the Serper API (https://serper.dev), returning the top-10 results with snippets of at most 150 characters. visit scrapes URL via crawl4ai (https: //github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai) and extracts the snippet most relevant to goal, ranked by ROUGE-L score (Lin, 2004). search(query): Performs web search: supply string query; the tool retrieves the top 10 results for the query. visit(url, goal): Visit webpage and return the relevant content based on the goal. A.3 Cost Calculation Let gin and gout denote the per-token input and output costs of language model, and let gs and gv denote the per-call costs of search and visit, respectively. The total cost per question is = cr + ct + ca. Rollout cost (cr): Following Yen et al. (2025), cached input tokens are excluded from the token count, as caching is standard in long-trajectory settings with shared context. The total (cid:17) rollout cost is cr = , where gin (i) in + gout (i) in = (i) in (i1) in (i) out (cid:16) . Tool call cost (ct): For BrowseComp-Plus, which uses local corpus, ct = 0. For all other tasks, ct = gs ns + gv nv, where ns and nv are the total numbers of search and visit calls. Aggregation cost (ca): Only LLM-based aggregation methods incur additional aggregation cost. For Solution Aggregation, ca is single LLM call: gin tin + gout tout. For Summary Aggregation, this is summed over + 1 calls. For AggAgent, ca is computed identically to cr, as it forms an agentic trajectory. Cost is averaged over all instances for each benchmark. Pricing rates are given in Table 3. Prices are sourced from the respective provider websites: GLM-4.7-Flash (https://docs.z .ai/guides/overview/pricing), Qwen3.5-122B (https://openrouter.ai/qwen/qwen3.5-1 22b-a10b), MiniMax-M2.5 (https://platform.minimax.io/docs/guides/pricing-paygo), and tool calls (https://www.firecrawl.dev/pricing).

Rollout latency (ℓr): Wall-clock time from the first step of the trajectory until termination. Aggregation latency (ℓa): For Solution Aggregation, the time of the single LLM call. For Summary Aggregation, we spawn summarization calls in parallel, wait for all to complete, then issue the final aggregation call; ℓa covers the full span. For AggAgent, ℓa is measured the same way as ℓr. For latency, we sample 30 instances and report the median for efficiency, as latency must be measured sequentially. All other experiments run with batched generation. sections.title: B Prompts
sections.content: Rollout agent We use the system prompt from Tongyi DeepResearch (Tongyi DeepResearch Team et al., 2025) (Figure 12), with an additional instruction from Dr. Tulu (Shao et al., 2025) appended for deep research tasks (Figure 13). For agentic search tasks, we format the user message following BrowseComp (Wei et al., 2025) (Figure 14). LLM-as-a-Judge For BrowseComp, BrowseComp-Plus, and HLE, we find that the original evaluation prompt occasionally produces false judgments, hence we instead use the prompt from Zhu et al. (2026). For ResearchRubrics, we find that the original prompt is unreliable for negative rubrics consistent with Hu et al. (2025) so we append clarifying section to the original prompt (Figure 15). For DeepSearchQA and Healthbench-Hard, we use the official evaluation prompts. LLM-based aggregation For Solution Aggregation and Summary Aggregation, we use the prompt from Li et al. (2025a). For AggAgent, we present the prompts in Figure 16 (agentic search) and Figure 17 (deep research), and the full tool descriptions in Figure 18. We vary the solution field description of the finish tool (Figure 19) in the following cases: 1) Deep research tasks: the model is instructed to generate long-form report. 2) Qwen3.5-122B: instructing the model to output in XML format sometimes causes tool parsing errors, so we use plain text format instead. This applies only to agentic search tasks.

sections.title: C Results
sections.content: C.1 Main Results We present the full results for Section 5.1 in Tables 5, 6, and 7. AggAgent achieves competitive performance not only at = 8 but also at = 2 and = 4. C.2 Cost and Latency vs. Performance We present the full results for Section 5.2 in Figures 8, 9, and 10. The per-benchmark results are consistent with the averaged figures in the main paper, with AggAgent achieving optimal performance relative to cost and latency. We also present the cost and latency breakdown in Tables 8, 9, and 10. Across all settings, AggAgent consistently incurs aggregation cost much below Summary Aggregation and comparable to Solution Aggregation. C.3 Stronger Models for Aggregation We present the full results for Section 6.1 in Table 11. While stronger aggregator (MiniMax) generally improves performance across all LLM-based aggregation methods, AggAgent remains the most effective. C.4 Confidence Calibration While prior work has demonstrated the effectiveness of confidence-based aggregation strategies such as Weighted Majority Voting and Best-of-N (Wei et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025a), we find that these methods do not consistently generalize across benchmarks. As illustrated in Figure 11, all three models show well-calibrated confidence on BrowseComp-Plus and BrowseComp (large gap), but are less calibrated on HLE and DeepSearchQA. Moreover, on Healthbench-Hard and ResearchRubrics, confidence is essentially uncorrelated with quality (r 0). We hypothesize that this miscalibration explains why confidence-based methods plateau or even degrade as increases on those tasks. In contrast, LLM-based aggregation, and especially AggAgent, scales reliably with across all benchmarks.

sections.title: Rollout agent system prompt for agentic search tasks
sections.content: You are deep research assistant. Your core function is to conduct thorough, multi-source investigations into any topic. You must handle both broad, open-domain inquiries and queries within specialized academic fields. For every request, synthesize information from credible, diverse sources to deliver comprehensive, accurate, and objective response. After you have gathered sufficient information, provide the definitive response and complete the task. Figure 12: Rollout agent system prompt for agentic search tasks, from Tongyi DeepResearch Team et al. (2025).

sections.title: Additional rollout agent system prompt for deep research tasks
sections.content: For the given question, please write comprehensive, evidence-backed answer to scientific questions. You should ground every nontrivial claim in retrieved snippets. Cite using <cite url="...">...</cite> drawn only from returned snippets. Please prefer authoritative sources (peer-reviewed papers, reputable benchmarks/docs) and prioritize recent work for fastmoving areas. You should acknowledge uncertainty and conflicts; if evidence is thin or sources disagree, state it and explain what additional evidence would resolve it. Its important to structure with clear markdown headers and coherent flow. In each section, write 2-5 sentence paragraphs with clear topic sentences and transitions; use lists sparingly only when they improve clarity. Ideally, you should synthesize rather than enumerate content: its helpful to group findings across papers, explain relationships, and build coherent narrative that answers the question, supported by citations. Most importantly, DO NOT invent snippets or citations and never fabricate content. At the end of your response, on new line, include your confidence score in the following format: Confidence: {{your confidence score between 0% and 100% for your answer}} Figure 13: Additional rollout agent system prompt for deep research tasks, from Shao et al. (2025).

sections.title: Rollout agent user message for agentic search tasks
sections.content: Your response should be in the following format: Explanation: {{your explanation for your final answer}} Exact Answer: {{your succinct, final answer}} Confidence: {{your confidence score between 0% and 100% for your answer}} Figure 14: Rollout agent user message for agentic search tasks, from Wei et al. (2025).

sections.title: Additional instruction for evaluating ResearchRubrics
sections.content: ## Handling Negative-Weight Criteria Some criteria describe **undesirable behaviors** and carry **negative weight**. The Weight field in the rubric indicates this. For these criteria: - **Satisfied (Score: 1.0)**: The undesirable behavior IS present in the document. This will reduce the overall score. - **Not Satisfied (Score: 0.0)**: The undesirable behavior is ABSENT from the document. This is the desired outcome and does not penalize the score. Your verdict must reflect whether the described behavior is literally present or absent in the document not whether the document is generally good or bad. Figure 15: Additional instruction for evaluating ResearchRubrics.

sections.title: AggAgent system prompt for agentic search tasks
sections.content: Identify the most reliable final solution based on verifiable tool observations, logical You are an aggregation agent. You are provided with task and set of candidate trajectories from independent agents that attempted to solve it. Your goal is to synthesize the most accurate, complete solution by drawing on the best reasoning and evidence across trajectories. You do NOT have access to the ground truth solution. **RESPONSIBILITIES** 1. Evaluate tool results and reasoning quality across all candidate trajectories. 2. consistency, and correct tool application. 3. If no single trajectory is fully reliable, synthesize corrected solution using only verified components from across trajectories. 4. Deliver your synthesized solution in the required format and provide justification. **REQUIRED PROCEDURE** You must follow these steps before calling finish. 1. **Survey the landscape** Read the TRAJECTORY METADATA in the user message. Identify which trajectories are worth inspecting based on step counts and patterns. 2. **Retrieve full solutions** Call get_solution (no arguments) to get the final content from every trajectorys last step, or pass trajectory_id to retrieve one specific trajectory. 3. **Verify with tool observations** Do not rely solely on final solutions or trajectorys own reasoning. For key claims or divergences, go back and inspect what the tools actually returned: - Use **search_trajectory**(trajectory_id, query) to locate steps where specific term or claim appears. Use role=tool to restrict to actual tool responses when verifying whether fact was directly observed this avoids misleading matches on agent reasoning. - Use **get_segment**(trajectory_id, start_step, end_step) to read contiguous range of steps (max 5). After finding relevant step via search, read it in full along with surrounding steps to see the raw tool output and surrounding context. 4. **Cross-check** Confirm: (a) tool observations in the log match what the agent claims, (b) reasoning is not circular, (c) arithmetic and logic are correct. **OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES** - **Tool results are ground truth; agent reasoning is not.** Within each trajectory, what tool *returned* is an objective observation. What the agent *concluded* from it is an interpretation that may be wrong. When in conflict, trust the tool output over the agents written reasoning about it. - **Count evidence, not trajectories.** single trajectory with clear, unambiguous tool observation supporting answer is stronger evidence than many trajectories that *reasoned* their way to without grounding in tool outputs. Majority agreement alone is not sufficient check what the tools actually showed. - **Identify Divergence:** Focus on steps where agents disagree. Determine which agents *observation from the environment* was correct, not which agent sounded more confident. - **Evidence Grounding:** Ensure tool observations directly support conclusions. If the log shows an error or empty result, the agent cannot validly claim success from that step. - **Quality over Confidence:** Prefer trajectories with validated, step-by-step reasoning over those that only state confident conclusion. **COMMON PITFALLS** - **Hallucinated Observations:** The agent claims tool returned X, but the log shows (or nothing). - **Silent Failures:** The agent receives an error but continues as if it succeeded. - **Circular Logic:** The agent assumes the answer before deriving it from data. - **Arithmetic/Logical Errors:** The data is correct, but the calculation or inference is flawed. - **Majority Bias:** Do not treat numerical agreement among trajectories as strong evidence. Many trajectories reaching the same conclusion via similar reasoning is weaker than one trajectory with concrete, verifiable tool result. **SOLUTION FORMAT (finish tool)** The solution argument must be single string with exactly two XML sections: <explanation>...</explanation><answer>...</answer>. - **CORRECT:** Self-contained. reader who never saw trajectories understands how the answer was derived. No mentions of "trajectory 1", get_solution", or "agent". Example: 29 <explanation>We need the 2020 population. The census table shows state with 1.2M and state with 0.8M; the question asks for the sum. 1.2 + 0.8 = 2.0 million.</explanation><answer>2.0 million</answer> - **WRONG:** "Trajectory 2 had the right answer so chose it." or "According to get_solution, the answer is 42." Do NOT reference trajectory IDs or tools in the solution. **TERMINATION** Call finish only after verifying key reasoning against actual tool outputs. Do not finish after only reading metadata or only get_solution; verify at least one critical claim with get_segment or search_trajectory when trajectories disagree. Figure 16: AggAgent system prompt for agentic search tasks.

sections.title: AggAgent system prompt for deep research tasks
sections.content: Identify the most reliable final solution based on verifiable tool observations, logical You are an aggregation agent. You are provided with task and set of candidate trajectories from independent agents that attempted to solve it. Your goal is to synthesize the most accurate, complete solution by drawing on the best reasoning and evidence across trajectories. You do NOT have access to the ground truth. **RESPONSIBILITIES** 1. Evaluate tool results and reasoning quality across all candidate trajectories. 2. consistency, and correct tool application. 3. If no single trajectory is fully reliable, synthesize corrected solution using only verified components from across trajectories. 4. Deliver your synthesized solution in the required format and provide justification. **REQUIRED PROCEDURE** You must follow these steps before calling finish. 1. **Survey the landscape** Read the TRAJECTORY METADATA in the user message. Identify which trajectories are worth inspecting based on step counts and patterns. 2. **Retrieve full solutions** Call get_solution (no arguments) to get the final content from every trajectorys last step, or pass trajectory_id to retrieve one specific trajectory. 3. **Verify with tool observations** Do not rely solely on final solutions or trajectorys own reasoning. For key claims or divergences, go back and inspect what the tools actually returned: - Use **search_trajectory**(trajectory_id, query) to locate steps where specific term or claim appears. Use role=tool to restrict to tool responses when verifying whether fact was directly observed. - Use **get_segment**(trajectory_id, start_step, end_step) to read contiguous range of steps (max 5). After finding relevant step via search, read it in full along with surrounding steps to see the raw tool output and surrounding context. 4. **Cross-check** Confirm: (a) tool observations in the log match what the agent claims, (b) reasoning is not circular, (c) arithmetic and logic are correct. 5. **Synthesize** Write unified response that: - Covers every important aspect addressed by any candidate - Takes the highest-quality treatment of each aspect (not just the most common) - Resolves contradictions by preferring more specific, better-supported, or more precise content - Reads as single coherent response, not patchwork **QUALITY CRITERIA** - **Completeness:** The synthesized response must be at least as comprehensive as the best individual candidate, and more comprehensive where candidates complement each other. - **Accuracy:** Prefer specific, precise content over vague generalizations. When candidates conflict, do not average choose the more defensible position. - **Coherence:** The final response must flow naturally. Integrate content rather than concatenating sections. - **Self-contained:** Do not mention trajectories, agents, candidates, or aggregation anywhere in the response. - **Citations:** Ground every nontrivial claim in retrieved snippets. Cite using <cite url="...">...</cite> drawn only from returned snippets; never fabricate URLs or content. 30 **COMMON PITFALLS** - **Cherry-picking the best-sounding candidate:** Length or fluency is not quality. shorter candidate may cover critical aspect better. - **Ignoring minority candidates:** single candidate covering an important aspect well outweighs many candidates that omit it. - **Concatenation instead of synthesis:** Stitching sections together without integrating them produces an incoherent response. Rewrite to unify. - **Contradiction averaging:** If candidates disagree, do not hedge reason about which is more accurate and commit to it. - **Omitting details:** If candidate covers subtopic with more depth, preserve that depth in the synthesis. **TERMINATION** Call finish with solution_report (your complete synthesized response) and reason (a concise account of how you combined the candidates and resolved any conflicts) after you have read and compared all candidates. Figure 17: AggAgent system prompt for deep research tasks.
